title: 10 best herbal teas for eye irritation

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Eyebright tea contains flavonoids and phenolic acids that have anti-inflammatory effects, which helps to reduce swelling and redness in the eyes.
Antioxidant properties: Eyebright tea's antioxidant compounds help protect the eyes from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to eye irritation and dryness.
Mucolytic properties: The saponins present in Eyebright tea have mucolytic activity, which means they can break down mucus and other debris that can accumulate in the eyes and cause irritation.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Euphrasia officinalis, also known as eyebright, teas helps with eye irritation because of its potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The bioactive compounds present in the plant, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, have been shown to reduce swelling and soothe red, itchy eyes. Eyebright teas have been traditionally used to treat conditions like conjunctivitis and dry eyes, promoting healthy eye function and reducing discomfort associated with eye irritation. This natural remedy offers a gentle and effective way to alleviate eye irritation.
remedy_constituents: Rutin: This flavonoid helps with eye irritation by reducing inflammation and improving blood vessel integrity, thereby alleviating swelling and redness associated with eye irritations.

Luteolin: As an anti-inflammatory and antioxidant compound, luteolin helps to soothe eye irritations by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation, thereby promoting a healthy environment for the eyes.
Euphrasperone: This terpene has shown antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to calm and soothe irritated eyes by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are rich in tannins and flavonoids, which help to reduce inflammation and soothe eye irritation.
Flowers: They contain bioactive compounds that have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making them effective in treating eye infections and irritation.
Roots: They are rich in alkaloids, which have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, helping to calm eye irritation and reduce inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Gather fresh or dried euphrasia officinalis leaves and flowers to use in the tea.
Measure out 10 to 20 grams of euphrasia officinalis leaves and flowers for a standard serving.
Boil 250 milliliters of water and let it cool for 5 minutes to avoid damaging the herbs.
Steep the euphrasia officinalis leaves and flowers in the cooled water for 5 to 10 minutes.
Strain the tea and discard the solids before serving.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh calendula officinalis flowers in the morning after dew has evaporated for optimal potency and flavor.
Gently dry the harvested flowers using a low-temperature dehydrator or air-drying method for 1 to 3 weeks.
Crush the dried flowers into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle for maximum release of properties.
Measure out 1 to 2 teaspoons of the dried calendula officinalis powder for every 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the calendula powder in the boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes to release the medicinal properties.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh matricaria chamomilla flowers at dawn when they are fully open for optimal potency.
Dry the flowers in a single layer on a paper bag or a food dehydrator at 100°f for 2 hours.
Store the dried flowers in airtight glass containers to preserve their medicinal properties and fragrance.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of the dried flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release their active compounds.
Strain the tea and let it cool before serving it hot or chilled as needed for relaxation and sleep.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Rosemary's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as camphor and bornyl acetate, can help reduce swelling and itching in the eyes, providing relief from conditions like conjunctivitis and blepharitis.

Antimicrobial properties: Rosemary's antimicrobial compounds, including rosmarinic acid, may help combat bacterial and fungal infections that can cause eye irritation, such as conjunctivitis and keratitis.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rosemary roots have been traditionally used in herbal remedies to soothe and calm irritated eyes, due to their anti-inflammatory properties.
Buds: Rosemary buds contain flavonoids and terpenes that help reduce redness and swelling in the eyes, making them a popular choice for eye irritation remedies.
remedy_recipe: Gather one tablespoon of dried rosmarinus officinalis leaves and place them in a tea infuser.
Boil fresh water in a kettle or pot for three to five minutes.
Pour the boiling water over the dried rosmarinus officinalis leaves and let it steep for seven to ten minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup, discarding the solids.
Allow the tea to cool before using as an eye rinse or applying warm compress.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Thyme teas helps with eye irritation because of its potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried thyme leaves or one tablespoon of fresh thyme leaves into a tea infuser or heat-resistant cup.
Bring filtered water to a boil and pour it over the thyme, allowing it to steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean cup, discarding the solids.
Allow the tea to cool to room temperature, which usually takes about 10-15 minutes depending on ambient temperature.
Use the cooled tea as an eyewash by gently pouring it over closed eyes and allowing it to drain off before opening.

remedy_recipe: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried lemon balm leaves and 1 tablespoon of dried chamomile flowers.
Heat 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes in a large teapot or kettle.
Add the dried herbs to the hot water and steep for 10-15 minutes to allow flavors and nutrients release.
Strain the tea into a separate container using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth.

remedy_recipe: Gather dried verbena officinalis leaves from a trusted source weighing approximately 2 tablespoons.
Bring one quart of filtered water to a boil in a large pot.
Steep the verbena leaves in boiling water for 5-7 minutes allowing the infusion to cool slightly.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean glass container.
Store the cooled tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to eye irritation that are treated with herbal teas are headaches, sinusitis, and skin irritations. The anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of herbs like peppermint, chamomile, and calendula can help alleviate symptoms such as redness, itchiness, and discomfort associated with these conditions. These teas can also provide relief from allergic reactions, eczema, and acne by soothing the affected area and reducing inflammation.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for eye irritation are a natural remedy used to soothe and calm irritated eyes. These teas are made from herbs that have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help to reduce redness, itching, and swelling in the eyes. Examples of herbal teas that help with eye irritation include peppermint, chamomile, and calendula. Drinking these teas can improve lives by providing relief from dry, itchy eyes, allowing individuals to go about their daily activities with comfort and ease.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with eye irritation is a blend of chamomile, calendula, and eyebright. Chamomile soothes and calms the eyes, while calendula reduces inflammation and promotes healing. Eyebright, rich in antioxidants, helps to reduce redness and discomfort. Together, these teas create a powerful synergy that can help to alleviate symptoms of eye irritation, such as dryness, itchiness, and redness. Drinking a cup of this tea combination can provide relief and promote healthy vision.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Oxidative medicine and cellular longevity, topical administration of antioxidant medicinal plant extracts can be beneficial for eye irritation due to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. In the study, eye drops containing extracts were applied to a mouse model of experimental dry eye, and the results showed significant improvements in clinical parameters such as tear volume, tear film break-up time, and corneal fluorescein staining scores. Specifically, the 0.1% extract treatment resulted in a notable decrease in inflammatory markers (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and IFN-γ levels) and oxidative stress (extracellular ROS production) in the ocular surface.
